data.title: U.S. stocks slide as oil prices continue to spike amid raging Iran war
data.description:
Investing.com - U.S. stocks slumped on Thursday after oil prices surged, as global moves to release record amounts of strategic crude reserves failed to quell investors anxious about tanker traffic disruptions due to the Iran conflict.
By 11:38 ET (15:38 GMT), the benchmark S&P 500 index slipped 1.2% to 6,692.52 points, the tech-heavy Nasdaq Composite shed 1.7% to 22,342.31 points, and the blue-chip Dow Jones Industrial Average slid 1.4% to 46,733.26 points.
The three main averages are coming off a mixed session on Wednesday, in which the Dow slumped to its lowest closing level so far this year. The S&P ended only marginally in the red, while the Nasdaq eked out a gain. Conflicting signals over a potential end to the war in the Middle East were offset by better-than-expected results from cloud-computing giant Oracle.    
[[pro_promotion |
Track U.S. stocks with InvestingPro
]]
New Iran leader says Hormuz strait to stay closed
Weighing on sentiment has been an effective stoppage to vessels passing through the Strait of Hormuz, a critical shipping choke point surrounded on three sides by Iran. Container companies, keen to protect crews and struggling to find insurance, have all but halted sailings in the narrow waterway that hosts a fifth of the world’s oil and liquefied natural gas supply.
"The Strait of Hormuz must remain closed," Iran’s official news outlet, the Islamic Republic News Agency, said, quoting the country’s new leader, Mojtaba Khamenei.
Merchant ships in and around the strait have been targeted by strikes, exacerbating fears over squeezed oil flows. On Wednesday, the United Kingdom Maritime Trade Operations, a maritime agency monitoring shipping activity, said a third vessel had be hit by an unknown projectile, after two other ships were hit and left on fire off of Iraq’s coast. Iraq and Oman have since moved to close oil terminals.
President Donald Trump on Thursday said on his Truth Social service that, with the U.S. being the largest oil producer in the world, "when oil prices go up, we make a lot of money."
Hormuz closure largest oil supply disruption ever
The International Energy Agency warned that the war in the Middle East was creating the largest supply disruption in the history of the global oil market. The IEA, which previously announced its largest-ever release of strategic oil reserves on Wednesday, slashed its annual supply outlook.
A now more than week-old joint U.S. and Israeli assault on Iran has led to massive fluctuations in prices in recent days and sparking concerns over reigniting inflationary pressures on countries around the world. Bets that central banks, such as the Federal Reserve, will reconsider possible interest rate cuts has pushed up bond yields, further denting the appeal of stocks.
Brent crude was last up 8.6% to $99.60 a barrel. Earlier this week, the global benchmark soared to as high as nearly $120 a barrel.
The Trump administration is looking into a potential waiver of the Jones Act of 1920 which governs domestic shipping to ease movement of fuel around the country, Reuters reported on Thursday, citing two sources familiar with the ​discussions.
Jobless claims headlines economic calendar
Thursday’s economic calendar was somewhat busy, highlighted by initial jobless claims. The number of Americans filing for unemployment claims in the past week fell to 212k, lower than the consensus. The soft reading comes after a weak February nonfarm payrolls report last week.
"Initial jobless claims were largely unchanged from the prior week, and — outside of a brief pop in late January and early February — have been broadly rangebound at a level lower than the average for most of 2025. Similarly, continuing jobless claims have also have been fluctuating within a fairly narrow range this year, one that is similar to the level at this time last year and well below its previously higher run rate during 2H25," JPMorgan’s Michael Hanson said.
"These data thus remain broadly consistent with a low-hire, low-fire equilibrium, and suggest a more benign state of the labor market than, say, the decline in February nonfarm payrolls," he said.
"The lack of a material and sustained rise in layoffs keeps alive the potential for a broader recovery in the labor market, although the recent jump in energy prices due to geopolitical events in the Middle East does present some downside risks to the growth and employment outlook," Hanson added.
Separately, data showed the U.S. trade deficit narrowed sharply in January, with exports jumping to a record high and imports falling.
Adobe earnings ahead
Beyond the war in Iran, traders were also eyeing upcoming results from Adobe, curious to see how the Photoshop-maker is grappling with recent concerns around AI’s impact on the software sector.
Once thought to be a industry tailwind, the emergence of new AI tools has fueled worries in recent months around potential disruptions that may upend a host of software-as-a-service companies. Investors have particularly been fretting that new flavors of the technology, such as plug-ins to Anthropic’s Claude agent that can pour through legal documents, could greatly demand for businesses ranging from data analytics to marketing.
The sector-specific S&P 500 Information Technology, which Adobe is a constituent of, has dropped by more than 3% so far this year. It has been a notable reversal from 2025, when the index notched a total return of 24%.
Shares of Adobe, whose suite of offerings include InDesign and Acrobat, have been reflective of this trend, sliding by more than 17% year-to-date.
Even before this year’s existential crisis in software, Adobe had been looking to head off these possible disruptions pursuing its own AI strategy, racing to fold the nascent technology into its services through tools like Firefly and Adobe Express. These enhancements aim to entice users by allowing them to quickly create images and videos within Adobe’s Creative Cloud.
The effort to monetize AI developments has appeared to place the company in a solid position, with executives forecasting full-year fiscal 2026 revenue and profit that have topped Wall Street expectations. The firm said it expects annual revenue to between $25.90 billion and $26.10 billion, while earnings per share are seen coming in at a range of $23.30 to $23.50.
Elsewhere, discount retailer Dollar General shares fell on an annual comparable sales forecast which underwhelmed Wall Street expectations.
Scott Kanowsky contributed to this article

data.title: Big bank capital to fall "small amount" under new plan, says Fed
data.description: By Pete Schroeder WASHINGTON, March 12 (Reuters) - Large bank capital requirements will fall slightly under revised drafts of sweeping bank capital rules, Federal Reserve Vice Chair for Supervision Michelle Bowman said Thursday, in a major victory for Wall Street lenders that had faced capital hikes under previous drafts. Speaking at the Cato Institute in Washington, Bowman outlined the changes to the so-called Basel rules and "GSIB surcharge" which determine how much money banks must set aside to absorb potential losses. In aggregate, the changes will lower large bank capital requirements by a "small amount" via a "sensible recalibration" of existing rules, said Bowman, who was appointed to the role last year by Republican President Donald Trump. Bowman said the changes would eliminate overlap and calibrate requirements to match actual risks, arguing a steady increase in capital in recent years has been misguided and harmful. "When capital requirements become excessive, they impair the banking system’s fundamental function of providing credit to the real economy," she said according to prepared remarks. The overhaul marks the culmination of a years-long effort by Wall Street banks to ease rules ⁠introduced following the 2007-09 financial crisis which they and the Trump-appointed regulators say are impeding lending and stymieing economic growth.  A Morgan Stanley research note this week said large banks currently have over $175 billion in excess capital, and clarity on the rules could allow them begin deploying that cash via lending and share buybacks. That expected outcome marks a dramatic turnaround for the industry, which faced a 19% increase when the proposal was first unveiled in 2023 by Bowman’s Democratic predecessor Michael Barr, sparking ​an unprecedented industry pushback. Bowman said Thursday that if her planned changes are adopted, banks capital will return to 2019 levels. Both rules, which are complex and lengthy, will be subject to industry feedback, and it is unclear when they may ultimately be finalized.

data.title: BoC expected to hold rates amid geopolitical volatility - BofA
data.description:
Investing.com - The Bank of Canada is projected to maintain its policy rate at 2.25% through the duration of 2026. According to a report from BofA Securities economist Carlos Capistran, persistent geopolitical tensions and trade uncertainties are expected to anchor the central bank’s current position.
Recent energy market surges following military operations in Iran have introduced significant upside risks to the Canadian outlook. "Higher oil prices increase growth and inflation in Canada, so it goes against rate cuts," Capistran posited regarding the shift in economic tailwinds.
[[pro_promotion | Find TSX stocks insulated from geopolitical volatility by upgrading to InvestingPro - get 50% off today]]
While the domestic economy signaled weakness with a GDP contraction in late 2025, the surge in crude prices acts as a structural offset. BofA thinks the central bank will adopt a cautious tone in its upcoming forward guidance to maintain policy flexibility.
The labor market also presents a complex narrative for policymakers after a mixed January performance. Capistran noted that "the unemployment rate fell to 6.5%, but this was driven by a drop in labor‑force participation rather than genuine job creation."
Inflation dynamics further complicate the path forward as core measures continue to trend downward toward the target. However, BofA warns that a sustained 10% increase in oil prices could lift headline inflation by approximately 40 basis points.
Market pricing has already begun to reflect this hawkish shift, moving away from previous expectations of rate relief. "The Canadian economy typically benefits from higher oil prices, and we now see the balance of risks shifting from BoC cuts in ‘26 to potential hikes," Capistran wrote.

data.title: Stellantis exploring deals for Chinese automakers to invest in its business, Bloomberg reports
data.description: March 12 (Reuters) - Stellantis is exploring deals with Chinese carmakers through which they would invest in the Fiat owner’s struggling European operations, Bloomberg News reported on Thursday. Its executives have met with China’s Xiaomi and Xpeng Inc to discuss options for an overhaul of Stellantis in Europe, including for them to acquire stakes in Maserati or other brands, the report said, citing people familiar with the matter. Reuters could not immediately verify the report.

data.title: Trump administration may loosen US shipping rules to combat fuel price spike, White House says
data.description: By Jarrett Renshaw WASHINGTON, March 12 (Reuters) - The Trump administration is considering waiving the century-old Jones Act for a limited period to ensure energy and agricultural shipments can move freely between U.S. ports, press secretary Karoline Leavitt said on Thursday, in a bid to combat Iran-related supply disruptions. "In the interest of national defense, the White House is considering waiving the Jones Act for a limited period of time to ensure vital energy products and agricultural necessities are flowing freely to U.S. ports," Leavitt said in a statement. "This action has not been finalized," she added. The announcement could come as early as Thursday, two sources familiar with the effort told Reuters, and would be aimed at combating spiking fuel prices and other disruptions since the start of the U.S.-Israeli war on Iran. High gasoline prices carry significant political risks for President Donald Trump and fellow Republicans, who have long argued that their energy policies would keep fuel affordable for American consumers. A sustained rise in pump prices could undermine that message and fuel criticism from Democrats that the administration has failed to shield households from higher costs, particularly as voters remain sensitive to inflation ahead of the November midterm elections. U.S. national average retail gasoline prices hit $3.60 a gallon on Thursday for the first time since May 2024, while diesel prices hit $4.89 a gallon, the highest since December 2022, data from the motorist association AAA showed. Under the Jones Act, goods shipped between U.S. ports must be carried on vessels that are U.S.-built, U.S.-flagged and mostly U.S.-owned. The requirement sharply limits the number of tankers available for domestic shipments. Waiving the rule temporarily would allow foreign ships to carry fuel between U.S. ports, potentially lowering shipping costs and speeding deliveries. The largest U.S. farm lobby, the American Farm Bureau Federation, asked Trump in a March 9 letter to waive the Jones Act to improve domestic transportation capacity, warning of fertilizer price spikes for farmers caused by shipping disruptions through the Strait of Hormuz. The United States has issued Jones Act waivers in the past only sparingly, typically in response to major supply disruptions. The most recent waivers came after hurricanes such as Hurricane Harvey and Hurricane Maria in 2017. At the time, the U.S. Department of Homeland Security temporarily allowed foreign-flagged vessels to transport fuel between U.S. ports to ease shortages and speed deliveries to affected regions.

data.title: Iran war casts shadow over HSBC and StanChart Middle East ambitions
data.description: By Lawrence White and Selena Li LONDON/HONG KONG, March 12 (Reuters) - Just days before the U.S. and Israel launched strikes on Iran, HSBC’s CEO Georges Elhedery said the Asia-Middle East corridor was becoming "a defining axis of global growth". This week, HSBC closed its Qatar branches while Standard Chartered evacuated its Dubai office and told staff there to work from home, in a sign of how the conflict has rattled their day-to-day activities and their ambitions. The duo, which have both bet on the region’s increasing trade with Asia and other markets to fuel their growth, are two of the global banks most exposed to the war with Iran, according to Reuters analysis of company data and sector analysts. While the share of their assets in the region is around 2%-3% of their global lending, that belies the strategic importance of the growing financial hubs of Dubai, Riyadh and Abu Dhabi to the trade-focused British banks. HSBC shares dropped more than 6% on Thursday, bringing the falls since the U.S. and Israel struck Iran on February 28 to 14%. StanChart shares are down about 11.4% against a 9.5% drop in the wider STOXX Europe banks index over that period. "Our network has proven to be adaptable and resilient, allowing us to stay close to our clients, respond quickly to their needs and continue enabling trade, capital, wealth and investment flows across our markets," a spokesperson for StanChart said of its Middle East exposure. HSBC referred to a statement from Elhedery this week, which said it remained confident in the region and its prospects. Other international banks including JPMorgan and Citigroup have been expanding in the Gulf too. JPMorgan’s financial exposure in the UAE doubled to $5.7 billion between 2024 and 2025, regulatory filings show, although that ranks the UAE as eighteenth in a list of the top 20 country exposures for the bank outside of the United States. Citigroup’s UAE exposure is bigger, at $17.3 billion at the end of 2025, but has been growing more slowly. Citigroup said on Thursday it would temporarily close most of its UAE branches and financial centres as a precautionary measure. CHINA-MIDDLE EASTERN TRADE StanChart, whose Middle East operations are based in Dubai, has seen its UAE business grow from 3.7% to 5.7% of overall group income in the last 5 years, an analysis of its statements shows, and its share of assets hold steady at around 2.4%. JPMorgan analysts on Thursday forecast Middle Eastern exposure for StanChart’s revenue and profit before tax to be about 8% and 12%, respectively, and for HSBC at about 4%. They said both banks were the most exposed among European lenders. Business volumes between China and the Middle East rose 18% in the last year, Manus Costello, StanChart’s global head of investor relations, told Reuters last month. That means any risks to inter-regional trade as the conflict shuts down airspace, hurts business confidence and stokes geopolitical tensions, could have an outsized impact. "We think the increased economic uncertainty could imply some additional risks related to the Groups’ trade finance and credit costs," Kathy Chan, equity analyst at Morningstar said. StanChart should be somewhat insulated from severe credit losses in the region because 73% of its UAE exposure is to government-related entities and banks, JPMorgan analysts added. Neither bank discloses its direct exposure to the Middle East, but StanChart’s UAE business and HSBC’s Saudi Arabia-based regional entity HSBC Bank Middle East can be used as proxies, the analysts said. The banks could also benefit from the disruption as it drives demand for services including foreign exchange and cash management, said Hargreaves Lansdown analyst Matt Britzman.

data.title: Is SentinelOne stock a buy before today’s earnings? D.A. Davidson weighs in
data.description: SentinelOne (S) will report its fiscal fourth-quarter results later today, with analysts assessing the stock setup ahead of the release.In a preview note, analysts at D.A. Davidson said recent industry feedback has turned more positive than expected."Channel feedback in our FQ4’26 VAR survey reversed again for S Q/Q & was better than we were expecting,” they noted, increasing confidence that SentinelOne could beat expectations for annual recurring revenue (ARR) in the quarter. The cybersecurity firm is due to release its results after the market close on March 12.The earnings report comes shortly after SentinelOne announced a leadership change. The company appointed Sonalee Parekh as chief financial officer, effective March 24. Parekh joins from Asana (ASAN), where she served as CFO since September 2024, and previously held senior finance roles at RingCentral (RNG) and Hewlett Packard Enterprise (HPE).The move follows a difficult stretch for SentinelOne as it operates in an increasingly competitive cybersecurity market, where artificial intelligence is rapidly transforming threat detection and endpoint protection.The company offers the AI-driven Singularity platform, which analyzes security data in real time, automates threat detection and response, and helps reduce manual workloads for security teams.SentinelOne competes with major industry players such as CrowdStrike (CRWD) and Palo Alto Networks (PANW), both of which hold leading positions in the cybersecurity space.Analysts argued that the timing of the CFO transition could shape how investors interpret the company’s first outlook for fiscal 2027 (FY27). Because Parekh will only join the company after the fourth-quarter results are reported, there is “some risk initial FY27 guidance will be a bit more conservative than it otherwise would be.” Management previously indicated that roughly $200 million in net new ARR would represent a reasonable starting point for fiscal 2027 expectations, a level broadly aligned with current consensus estimates.This guidepost will likely be reiterated "but we would not be shocked if it is lowered a bit given the timing of when the new CFO is joining," analysts wrote. Analysts also made slight adjustments to forward estimates. Fiscal 2027 revenue is now projected at about $1.193 billion, representing roughly 19.2% year-over-year growth and slightly below prior estimates, while operating margin expectations were trimmed modestly to about 6.6%. They continue to rate SentinelOne shares Neutral and lowered the price target to $14 from $16. The analysts said the stock could eventually re-rate significantly higher if the company stabilizes ARR growth while continuing to improve margins, but added that clearer evidence of stronger sales execution and net new ARR (NNARR) expansion is still needed."We thus continue to await a more attractive entry point," they concluded.

data.title: RBC downgrades Vale S.A. as shares re-rated faster than its mining peers
data.description:
Investing.com - RBC downgraded Vale S.A. to Sector Perform from Outperform, saying the stock now appears fairly valued after a recent re-rating that has outpaced major mining peers.
The brokerage raised its price target to $15.50 per ADR from $14.50, reflecting higher forecasts for copper and nickel prices.[[pro_promotion |
Track every rating & target change with InvestingPro
]]
RBC said Vale’s shares have risen about 15% so far this year, slightly ahead of diversified mining peers. The stock has re-rated faster than rivals BHP Group and Rio Tinto across key valuation metrics despite a recent pullback tied to geopolitical tensions in the Middle East.
According to RBC, Vale’s valuation now implies an iron ore price of about $85 per tonne, compared with roughly $83 for Rio Tinto and about $91 for BHP. The brokerage said that level is about 10% higher than when it upgraded the stock previously.
RBC said shareholder returns remain attractive, estimating Vale’s dividend yield at about 8% for 2026, nearly double the roughly 4.8% average among peers. Lower capital spending and a solid balance sheet support the higher payout, the firm added.
The brokerage also pointed to potential from Vale’s base metals division as a longer-term catalyst, though it said the business remains underappreciated by investors. RBC said an initial public offering of the division could unlock value but recent management commentary suggests the company does not view such a transaction as urgent.
RBC raised its earnings outlook slightly after updating commodity price assumptions. Higher copper and nickel prices offset the impact of a stronger Brazilian real, leading the brokerage to lift its adjusted EBITDA forecast for 2026 through 2028 by about 2%.
RBC said Vale could face greater risks than some peers if geopolitical tensions persist, citing the company’s higher cost structure, exposure to pellet premiums and broader emerging market risks.

data.title: Iran’s leader says Strait of Hormuz should stay closed
data.description:
Investing.com - Iran’s new Supreme Leader Ayatollah Mojtaba Khamenei said the Strait of Hormuz should remain closed, marking his first public statement since taking power following his father’s assassination.
[[pro_promotion |
Unlock the hottest news by upgrading to InvestingPro - get 50% off today
]]
The comments were published Thursday on Iranian state television and indicate the new leader intends to maintain the blockade of the vital waterway.
Khamenei thanked the military and urged a record turnout at rallies across Iran. He stated that Iran would not give up on avenging an attack on a school.
"We believe in friendship with our neighbors," Khamenei said, adding that Iran only targets bases and will continue to do so.
The Supreme Leader said Iran will seek compensation from its enemies and called on regional countries to close U.S. bases. He also warned that other fronts will be opened if the war persists. 
President Donald Trump commented on the situation Thursday on Truth Social, writing that the United States is the largest oil producer in the world and benefits when oil prices rise. However, Trump said his greater priority as president is preventing Iran from obtaining nuclear weapons and destabilizing the Middle East and the world. "I won’t ever let that happen," Trump wrote.

data.title: Goldman raises U.S. PCE forecast, cuts GDP outlook on higher oil prices
data.description:
Investing.com - Goldman Sachs has lifted its U.S. inflation forecasts and trimmed its growth outlook for 2026, warning that the jump in oil prices tied to the war with Iran is now the primary channel of economic risk.
[[pro_promotion |
Go deeper with analyst-driven data on InvestingPro
]]
Goldman analyst Manuel Abecasis told clients in a note this week that the bank’s commodity strategists expect Brent crude to average $98 in March and April, “up 40% from the 2025 average,” before easing back to $71 by the fourth quarter of 2026. 
In an upside scenario in which flows through the Strait of Hormuz are disrupted for a full month, the bank estimates Brent would average $110 in March and April.
“Our rules of thumb are that a sustained 10% increase in oil prices boosts headline and core PCE inflation by 0.2pp and 0.04pp, respectively, while lowering GDP growth by around 0.1pp,” Abecasis wrote.
On the back of its revised oil assumptions and the latest CPI data, Goldman has raised its December 2026 headline PCE forecast by 0.8 percentage point to 2.9%, and lifted its core PCE projection by 0.2 point to 2.4%. 
It now expects 2026 GDP growth of 2.2% on a Q4-to-Q4 basis, down 0.3 point from its prior estimate.
The bank also warns that geopolitical risk historically depresses hiring and investment “in ways that extend beyond the effects of higher oil prices.” Goldman now sees the unemployment rate peaking at 4.6%.
A tougher inflation path means the Federal Reserve may wait longer to ease. The first cut is now expected in September rather than June, with a second in December, though Goldman noted that a weaker labor market could still pull cuts forward.
